structured_choices.data_source: Production session logs from a fashion e-commerce marketplace spanning 18 months, including timestamped impression, click, and add-to-cart events. Product catalog with titles, descriptions, category metadata (leaf category assigned at listing time), and image URLs. New products defined as items with <50 total impressions in their first 30 days post-listing. Cold-start evaluation restricted to products ≤90 days old at evaluation time.
structured_choices.label_definition: Binary implicit feedback label: click within session = positive (1), impression without click = negative (0). Session defined as consecutive events from the same user_id with ≤30 minute gaps. Ranking task: given a session context (prior clicks/cart events) and a candidate product shown in an impression, predict whether the user will click that product. Negative examples are sampled stratified by candidate product age-group and category to prevent bias toward old, popular items.
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal split by session timestamp. Training: months 1–12. Validation: months 13–15. Test: months 16–18. Justification: fashion has strong seasonality and trend effects; temporal split prevents information about future demand patterns from leaking into training. Cold-start boundary is respected by training the model on months 1–12 data but restricting evaluation (validation and test) to impressions of products that were ≤90 days old at the time of evaluation. This ensures the test set is truly cold-start and not conflating warm-start improvements with multimodal signal.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (text tokenizer, image encoder, categorical encoders, numeric scalers if any) are fit exclusively on the training set (months 1–12). Text: vocabulary and BPE tokenizer fit on training product titles and descriptions only. Image embeddings: pretrained ResNet50 (frozen; not fine-tuned, to isolate text/sequence signal) applied consistently. Categorical features (category, brand): ordinal encoder fit on training set, applied to validation and test. Sequence features (user click history, cart history): truncated to last 20 events, fit statistics (mean sequence length) computed on training set. No leakage from val/test labels into any preprocessing.
structured_choices.features: Product features: (1) Title text (tokenized, max 32 tokens), (2) Description text (tokenized, max 128 tokens), (3) Image embedding (2048-dim ResNet50 output), (4) Leaf category (ordinal encoded, 1200 categories), (5) Days since listing (numeric, log-scaled). Session/sequence features: (6) Last 20 user click events (each click is a product ID embedding + timestamp offset, concatenated), (7) Last 20 add-to-cart events (same format), (8) Session length so far (numeric). Interaction features: none that directly encode the label. All sequence embeddings are learnable, not pretrained.
structured_choices.model: Transformer-based multitask architecture: (A) Separate embedding layers for product title and description (shared vocab). (B) Product image embedding (frozen ResNet50 output, projected to 512 dim). (C) Concatenate product embeddings (title, desc, image, category, listing_age) → 1024 dim via linear projection. (D) Separate embedding layer for user sequence (click and cart events interleaved). (E) Multi-head self-attention over user sequence (8 heads, 512 dim, 2 layers) with absolute positional encoding. (F) Cross-attention: product representation attends to user sequence representation (4 heads, 512 dim, 1 layer). (G) Output: concatenate product representation, attended sequence representation, feed through 2-layer MLP (512→256→128) with dropout (0.2), sigmoid output for binary classification. Trained end-to-end with binary cross-entropy loss.
structured_choices.baseline: Gradient boosted tree model (XGBoost) trained on identical train set with hand-engineered features: (1) TF-IDF vectors of product title/description (top 200 features selected by variance on training set), (2) One-hot category, (3) Listing age, (4) Aggregate user sequence statistics: count of prior clicks, count of prior carts, recency of last click, diversity of prior categories clicked. No image features (image is multimodal signal being tested). Baseline receives equal hyperparameter tuning effort: grid search over learning_rate ∈ {0.01, 0.05, 0.1}, max_depth ∈ {4, 6, 8}, n_estimators ∈ {100, 500, 1000} on validation set. This ensures the baseline is not straw-manned; it is a strong, well-tuned non-neural model that isolates the hypothesis to the multimodal transformer, not to deep learning in general.
structured_choices.primary_metric: NDCG@10 (Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain at rank 10) over all cold-start impressions in the test set, computed per-user-session and then micro-averaged. Justification: (1) Ranking metric appropriate for learning-to-rank task; (2) NDCG@10 reflects the business goal of surface relevant items in top positions where users are most likely to engage; (3) Normalized form accounts for varying ground-truth clicks per session; (4) Cold-start restriction ensures measurement on items with minimal prior feedback, isolating multimodal signal from popularity bias. Secondary ranking metric: MAP@10 (Mean Average Precision) to verify NDCG results and check for sensitivity to ranking depth.
secondary_metrics: Click-through rate (CTR) on cold-start impressions: raw proportion of impressions that result in clicks. Lower variance, easier to interpret, but less sensitive to ranking quality.
NDCG@10 stratified by product age (0–30 days, 31–60 days, 61–90 days) to detect whether multimodal signal degrades with age and whether benefit is uniform across cold-start window.
NDCG@10 stratified by category (top 10 by volume, 'other') to detect whether multimodal improvements are category-specific or general, and to surface category imbalance confounds.
AUC-ROC on the full test set (not just cold-start; diagnostic only) to check for calibration issues or train-test distribution shift.
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Validation set (months 13–15) is used exclusively for hyperparameter tuning and early stopping. Both model and baseline are trained on months 1–12 and evaluated on months 13–15 with the same metrics (NDCG@10, CTR, stratified breakdowns). The hyperparameter configuration achieving the highest NDCG@10 on validation is locked in; no further tuning or re-training on test data. Test set (months 16–18) is held out and touched only once, at the very end, to measure final performance. This guards against overfitting to the validation set.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set is held completely out of the model development and selection loop. No hyperparameter tuning, no threshold optimization, no feature engineering is done using test set information. Final model and baseline are trained once on months 1–12, validated on months 13–15 to select hyperparameters, and then applied without modification to the test set (months 16–18). Test set results are reported as the primary claim of the hypothesis.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Popularity bias: (1) Baseline includes category and aggregate interaction features that capture popularity priors; if the transformer outperforms the baseline despite controlling for popularity, it is not merely rediscovering popularity. (2) Evaluation restricted to cold-start products (≤90 days old) which have inherently low interaction counts, reducing the confound. (3) Negative sampling during training is stratified by product age and category to prevent the model from learning category-level or age-level shortcuts. Seasonality and trend: temporal split (train 1–12, val 13–15, test 16–18) isolates test performance from training, so seasonal patterns in months 16–18 cannot be leaked from training labels. Category imbalance: stratified metrics (NDCG@10 by category) reveal whether gains are driven by over-representation of certain categories in the transformer's learned features. Exposure bias: NDCG, CTR, and other ranking metrics are computed over logged impressions only (what users saw), not over counterfactual missing data, so no unobserved feedback is assumed. The implicit feedback model (click = 1, no click = 0) is standard for this setting and does not require correction (e.g., inverse propensity weighting) because both models are trained on identical data.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: Multimodal Transformer vs. Text-Only Baseline for Cold-Start Product Recommendation

### 1. Data Source and Label Definition

We would use 18 months of production session logs from the fashion e-commerce marketplace. The dataset includes timestamped impression, click, and add-to-cart events; product catalog records (title, description, category, image URL); and user anonymized session IDs. A **session** is defined as consecutive events from the same user with ≤30 minute gaps between events.

The **label** is implicit feedback: for each (user_session, product_candidate) pair shown in an impression, we assign label = 1 if the user clicked the product within the session, and label = 0 otherwise. We define **cold-start products** as items ≤90 days old at the time of evaluation. This ensures the test set measures multimodal signal on truly sparse-interaction items, not on products that have accumulated click history.

### 2. Temporal Split Strategy

Data is split chronologically:
- **Training**: months 1–12
- **Validation**: months 13–15
- **Test**: months 16–18

**Justification**: Fashion e-commerce is subject to strong seasonality and trend effects. A temporal split prevents future demand patterns from leaking into the training set. Specifically, a product's popularity in month 18 (test time) is influenced by trends and seasonal demand unknown during months 1–12 (training); a random split would allow the model to exploit this future information indirectly. The temporal boundary ensures that the model learned on months 1–12 is genuinely predictive of months 16–18 behavior, not merely memorizing seasonal patterns. Additionally, all evaluation (validation and test) is restricted to impressions of products that are in their cold-start window (≤90 days old) at evaluation time, even if the product was listed earlier. This prevents the test set from conflating multimodal signal improvements with warm-start (high-interaction) improvements.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

**Preprocessing fit point**: All transformers are fit on the training set only (months 1–12) and applied without re-fitting to validation and test sets.

- **Text features**: Product title and description are tokenized using a byte-pair encoding (BPE) tokenizer fit on training set vocabulary. Title is truncated to 32 tokens; description to 128 tokens. Vocabulary is frozen for validation and test.
- **Image features**: Pre-trained ResNet50 embeddings (2048 dim) are computed for all product images and frozen (not fine-tuned). This isolates the hypothesis to text and sequence information; if the transformer outperforms, it is not due to fine-tuning image encoders on this task.
- **Categorical features**: Leaf category (≈1200 unique values) is ordinal-encoded using an encoder fit on training set. Unknown categories at test time are mapped to a reserved token.
- **Numeric features**: Days since product listing is log-scaled (fit: mean and std computed on training set). Sequence statistics (e.g., average prior click count per user) are normalized using training set statistics.
- **Sequence features**: User click and add-to-cart histories are truncated to the last 20 events each. Timestamps are converted to relative offsets from the current event. All event embeddings are learnable (not pretrained).

**No leakage**: No feature is derived from the test label or future sessions. Sequence features are strictly historical (prior to the current impression).

### 4. Model Architecture and Baseline

**Proposed Model** (Multimodal Transformer):
The model jointly encodes product features (text title, description, image embedding, category, listing age) and user session history (click and cart sequences). Architecture: separate embedding layers for title and description feed into a 1024-dim product representation. The image embedding (frozen ResNet50 output) is projected to 512 dim and concatenated. User click and cart sequences are embedded and processed through multi-head self-attention (8 heads, 2 layers, 512 dim) with absolute positional encoding. A cross-attention layer (4 heads, 1 layer) allows the product representation to attend to the user sequence. The attended representation is concatenated with the product representation and fed through a 2-layer MLP (512→256→128 dim, dropout 0.2) with sigmoid output for binary classification. The model is trained end-to-end with binary cross-entropy loss.

**Baseline** (Gradient Boosted Trees):
An XGBoost model trained on hand-engineered features derived from the same training data: TF-IDF vectors of product title/description (top 200 features selected by variance on training set), one-hot category, listing age, and aggregated user sequence statistics (prior click count, prior cart count, recency of last click, diversity of prior categories clicked). **Critically, the baseline does not use image features**; image is the multimodal signal being tested. The baseline is tuned with grid search over learning_rate ∈ {0.01, 0.05, 0.1}, max_depth ∈ {4, 6, 8}, n_estimators ∈ {100, 500, 1000}, evaluated on the validation set. This ensures the baseline is a strong, well-tuned comparison, not a straw man.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric**: NDCG@10 (Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain at rank 10) computed over all cold-start impressions (products ≤90 days old) in the test set, micro-averaged across sessions.

**Justification**: (1) NDCG@10 directly measures ranking quality—the business goal is to surface relevant items in top-10 positions where users are most likely to engage. (2) Normalization controls for varying ground-truth clicks per session. (3) Cold-start restriction isolates multimodal signal from popularity bias: if the model outperforms on sparse-interaction items, the gain is not simply rediscovering popularity. (4) The @10 cutoff reflects typical UI constraints (users scan top-10 recommendations).

**Secondary metrics**:
- **MAP@10**: Mean Average Precision, alternative ranking metric to verify robustness to metric choice.
- **CTR (cold-start)**: Click-through rate on cold-start impressions; lower variance, easier to interpret operationally.
- **NDCG@10 stratified by product age**: (0–30 days, 31–60 days, 61–90 days) to detect whether multimodal gains degrade with product age.
- **NDCG@10 stratified by category**: Top 10 categories by volume + 'other', to detect category-specific effects and confounding from category imbalance.
- **AUC-ROC (full test set, diagnostic only)**: to check for calibration issues or train-test distribution shift.

### 6. Model Selection and Validation

Both the proposed model and baseline are trained on months 1–12. Validation (months 13–15) is used exclusively to select hyperparameters and tune stopping criteria for both models, evaluated on NDCG@10. No further training or tuning is performed on the test set. The test set (months 16–18) is held out completely and evaluated only once, at the end, to report final performance.

### 7. Controls for Confounds

**Popularity bias**: The baseline includes category and aggregate user interaction features that capture popularity priors. If the transformer outperforms the baseline despite these controls, the gain is not simply popularity rediscovery. Additionally, evaluation is restricted to cold-start products, which inherently have low interaction counts.

**Category imbalance**: Stratified secondary metrics reveal whether gains are driven by over-representation of specific categories. If the transformer gains are concentrated in a few categories, the generality of the multimodal signal is questionable.

**Seasonality and trend**: Temporal split prevents the model from leaking future demand patterns into training. The test set reflects the true held-out temporal window.

**Negative sampling strategy**: During training, negative examples (impressions without clicks) are sampled stratified by product age-group and category, preventing the model from learning category or age shortcuts.

### 8. Claims and Scope

This design would establish whether a transformer jointly encoding product text, images, and session sequences outperforms a text-only baseline on cold-start ranking quality, measured by NDCG@10 on held-out test data. It would **not** establish: the sensitivity of the model to image quality or to the specific frozen image encoder choice (ResNet50 vs. others); whether the gains generalize to non-cold-start items; whether the model is efficient enough for production latency; or whether multimodal signal is valuable for other tasks (e.g., add-to-cart prediction). These are separate questions requiring separate experiments.
